issues.description: The dramatized claim that a student 'Scored zero on all three' parts for a missing unit, a missing 'BECAUSE', and a missing setup integral overstates the rubric. On real AP FRQ rubrics those omissions cost the specific point(s) (the units point, the justification point, the setup point), but a correct numerical answer often still earns the answer point. 'Zero on all three' is rhetorically punchy but not literally how partial-credit scoring works.
issues.fix: Soften to e.g. 'lost the easy points' or 'dropped a point on each' rather than 'scored zero on all three,' or explicitly frame it as a worst-case cautionary exaggeration so students don't infer one omission always zeroes a whole part.

issues.description: Statement 'The calculator answer alone earns zero' is a useful heuristic but is a slight overstatement for the same reason as the hook: a bare correct numerical answer with no setup typically loses the setup point, not necessarily all points on the part.
issues.fix: Phrase as 'the calculator answer alone usually loses the setup point' to stay strictly rubric-accurate while keeping the strong habit message.

notes: All exam-structure numbers (45 MC / 105 min / 50%, Part A 30 / 60 min no-calc, Part B 15 / 45 min calc; 6 FRQ / 90 min / 50%, Part A 2 / 30 min calc, Part B 4 / 60 min no-calc, 195 min = 3h15m total), the 90/6 = 15 min budget, the v = t^2 - 4 displacement (-3) and total distance (23/3), the FTC max-at-x=3 justification, the three-decimal and store-don't-retype calculator rules, and the units conventions are all correct; only two minor rhetorical overstatements of partial-credit scoring.
